name: Sort then Sweep
summary: Sort by start, then sweep once: if the next interval overlaps the last merged one, stretch its end; otherwise start a new merged interval.
visualizationIntent: init: the intervals sort into ascending order and an empty merged list appears. loop: pointer i sweeps the sorted intervals; each one becomes current. check: compare the current interval's start to the last merged interval's end. update: a non-overlapping interval is appended to merged (a new cell pops in); an overlapping one stretches the last merged interval's end (its cell flashes). return: merged holds the non-overlapping cover.

lineExplanations.3: Sort the intervals so overlapping ones sit next to each other, ordered by start.
lineExplanations.4: Create an empty list to hold the merged, non-overlapping intervals.
lineExplanations.5: Sweep through every interval in sorted order.
lineExplanations.6: Read the start of the current interval.
lineExplanations.7: Read the end of the current interval.
lineExplanations.8: If nothing has been merged yet, this is the first interval.
lineExplanations.9: Add the first interval to the merged list as-is.
lineExplanations.11: Look at the end of the most recently merged interval.
lineExplanations.12: If the current interval starts after that end, the two do not overlap.
lineExplanations.13: No overlap, so start a fresh merged interval.
lineExplanations.15: They overlap; only stretch the merged interval if the current one reaches further right.
lineExplanations.16: Extend the last merged interval's end to cover the current interval.
lineExplanations.17: Return the merged list of non-overlapping intervals.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: defines the class wrapper LeetCode expects.
syntaxExplanations.2: def merge(self, intervals): defines the method receiving the list of intervals.
syntaxExplanations.3: intervals.sort() sorts the list in place; pairs sort by first element then second.
syntaxExplanations.5: for i in range(len(intervals)): loops i over every interval index.
syntaxExplanations.6: intervals[i][0] reads the start of interval i.
syntaxExplanations.7: intervals[i][1] reads the end of interval i.
syntaxExplanations.8: if len(merged) == 0: is true when nothing has been merged yet.
syntaxExplanations.9: merged.append([start, end]) adds a copy of the interval.
syntaxExplanations.11: merged[-1][1] reads the end of the last merged interval.
syntaxExplanations.12: if start > last_end: is true when the current interval is disjoint.
syntaxExplanations.13: merged.append([start, end]) starts a new merged interval.
syntaxExplanations.15: if end > last_end: is true when the current interval extends further right.
syntaxExplanations.16: merged[-1][1] = end stretches the last merged interval's end.
syntaxExplanations.17: return merged hands back the merged intervals.
